title: Coffee and walnut traybake
subtitle: A classic British combination in a simple square format
excerpt: A light coffee sponge with walnuts throughout, baked in a single tin and topped with coffee buttercream. All the flavour of the classic layer cake without the assembly.
sourceNotes: Coffee and walnut cake is one of the most consistently documented recipes in British baking from the mid-twentieth century onward. It appears in Marguerite Patten's Cookery in Colour (1960), Mary Berry's Baking Bible (1994), and Delia Smith's Complete Cookery Course (1978), where it is treated as a foundational layered sponge. The traybake format, which reduces the recipe to a single tin and skips the layering, became popular through WI (Women's Institute) baking collections from the 1970s.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the buttercream, wrapped in cling film, for up to 2 months. Ice after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge base keeps for 2 days wrapped at room temperature. Ice on the day of serving.

baking.decoratingTechnique: coffee buttercream spread over cooled sponge, walnut halves to finish

content.text: Instant coffee dissolved in a small amount of hot water gives a cleaner, more direct coffee flavour than espresso in a sponge batter. The water evaporates during baking; the coffee compounds stay. Stir the dissolved coffee into the butter mixture before adding flour.

Toast the walnuts for 8 minutes at 170°C before chopping and adding to the batter. Toasting removes bitterness and brings out the oils, making the walnut flavour distinct rather than just a textural element.

attrs.prepNote: 2 tsp, dissolved in 2 tbsp hot water
dissolved in 1 tbsp hot water, cooled

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease and line a 20 x 30 cm rectangular tin. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy, about 4 minutes. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in the {{plain-flour}} and {{baking-powder}}, then stir in the dissolved {{instant-coffee}} and {{walnuts}}. Spread evenly into the tin. Bake for 28 to 32 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack to cool completely.

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} until smooth. Add the {{icing-sugar}} in two additions and beat well. Stir in the cooled dissolved {{instant-coffee}}. Spread the buttercream over the cooled sponge in an even layer. Press walnut halves from the remaining {{walnuts}} across the surface at regular intervals. Leave to set for 30 minutes before cutting into squares.
